qEnglish: If she ..... the instructions carefully, she ..... this mistake and ..... better marks.
optionsEnglish: reads / will not make / will get
read / would not make / would get
has read / does not make / gets
had read / would not have made / would have got
explanationEnglish: Third conditional (Past Perfect in if-clause, would have + V3 in main clause) is used for hypothetical past situations.
subject: English

qEnglish: If it ..... tomorrow, we ..... the match and ..... very disappointed.
optionsEnglish: had rained / would have cancelled / would have been
explanationEnglish: First conditional (Simple Present in if-clause, Simple Future in main clause) is used for real possibilities.
subject: English

qEnglish: If I ..... you, I ..... such a hasty decision and ..... more carefully about it.
optionsEnglish: were / would not take / would think
had been / would not have taken / would have thought
was / shall not take / am thinking
explanationEnglish: Second conditional (Past subjunctive 'were' in if-clause, 'would' in main clause) is used for imaginary present situations.
subject: English

qEnglish: Had she ..... earlier, she ..... the flight and ..... the important meeting.
optionsEnglish: left / would have caught / would have attended
explanationEnglish: Inverted third conditional (Had + Subject + V3) follows the same rule as third conditional.
subject: English

qEnglish: If water ..... to zero degrees, it ..... ice and ..... its original shape.
optionsEnglish: had cooled / would become / would retain
will cool / will become / will retain
explanationEnglish: Zero conditional (Simple Present in both clauses) is used for universal truths.
subject: English

qEnglish: If he were to reach the venue in time, he _______ have to hurry up.
explanationEnglish: This is a conditional clause using the subjunctive form 'were to', which requires 'would + base verb' in the main clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: If he _______, what are we to do?
explanationEnglish: This represents a zero/first conditional question framework, using the simple present 'comes' in the conditional clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: If you _______ a skeleton in the cellar, don't mention it to anyone.
explanationEnglish: This is a type 1 conditional structure mixed with an imperative main clause, calling for the simple present form 'find' in the if-clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: If they _______ that picture lower, people would be able to see it.
explanationEnglish: The presence of 'would + be' in the main clause tells us this is a Type 2 conditional sentence, which takes the simple past ('hung') in the conditional clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: They are very kind to me. They treat me _______ their own son.
explanationEnglish: The expression 'as if' when dealing with hypothetical or imaginary conditions requires the past subjunctive form 'were'.
subject: English

qEnglish: If he sticks to his exercise regime, he _______ his ideal weight by the end of this year.
explanationEnglish: This is a first conditional sentence. The if-clause is in the simple present ('sticks'), so the main clause requires the simple future ('will reach').
subject: English

qEnglish: If he _______ (work) harder, he would have succeeded.
explanationEnglish: This is a Type 3 conditional structure. The main clause contains 'would have + V3', meaning the conditional clause must feature the past perfect tense 'had worked'.
subject: English

qEnglish: Choose the correct sentence from the options below:
optionsEnglish: When I woke up, he has already eaten breakfast.
When I woke up, he had already eaten breakfast.
When I had woken up, he had already ate breakfast.
When I had woken up, he has already ate breakfast.
explanationEnglish: When sequencing two events in the past, the earlier event takes the past perfect ('had already eaten') and the later event takes the simple past ('woke up').
subject: English

qEnglish:
Match the items in column (A) with appropriate items in column (B) to form meaningful sentences:
Column (A):
(a) If you had tried
(b) Unless you tell me the truth
(c) If you are going abroad....
(d) Do you know

Column (B):
(i) ......whether the match has been postponed.
(ii) you'll need a lot of money.
(iii) I'll not let you go.
(iv) you would have succeeded.
explanationEnglish: Clause (a) is third conditional and pairs with (iv); clause (b) requires a future condition, matching (iii); clause (c) matches (ii) logically; phrase (d) introduces a indirect question matching (i).
subject: English

qEnglish:
Match the parts of sentences listed in Column (A) with those in Column (B) to frame appropriately meaningful sentences:
Column (A):
(a) Unless you work hard
(b) If you were rich
(c) The boy who stood first....
(d) We reached the station......

Column (B):
(i) you would probably spend much
(ii) got the prize.
(iii) .... before the train arrived.
(iv) you will fail.
explanationEnglish: (a) links with future consequence (iv); second conditional (b) links with (i); clause subject (c) links with active predicate (ii); past timeline (d) links with relative tracking phrase (iii).
subject: English

qEnglish: Fill in the blank with the correct conjunction: He commands me _______ he were my boss.
explanationEnglish: The use of the past subjunctive 'were' indicating an imaginary condition shows that the phrase must be introduced by 'as if'.
subject: English

qEnglish: I was no better than a dead man, _______ I was alive and was able to reach the shore.
explanationEnglish: The contrastive concessive conjunction 'Though' perfectly connects the vulnerability of the situation to the survival resolution.
subject: English

qEnglish: Which of the following structures of a question is correct?
optionsEnglish: Will be they arrested if they refuse to leave?
Will they be arrested if they refuse to leave?
Will they be arrested if they refused to leave?
Will they be arrested if they will refuse to leave?
explanationEnglish: The interrogative passive voice structure follows 'Will + subject + be + V3', combined with a standard type-1 simple present if-clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: Choose the grammatically correct option among the four given sentences:
optionsEnglish: Had you not misbehaved with your superiors, you would not have come to this sorry pass.
Had you not misbehaved with your superiors, you might not come to this sorry pass
Had you not misbehaved with your superiors, you should not come to this sorry pass.
Had you not misbehaved with your superiors, You would not came to this sorry pass.
explanationEnglish: An inverted Type 3 conditional construction correctly uses 'Had + subject + V3' coupled with 'would not have + V3' in its corresponding main clause.
subject: English

qEnglish:
Choose the part of the sentence that is grammatically incorrect:
_Supposing if_ (A) / _he misses the train_ (B) / _will_ (C) / _he come back_ (D)?
explanationEnglish: Using 'Supposing' and 'if' together creates a grammatical redundancy. Only one conditional connector is needed to introduce the scenario.
subject: English

qEnglish:
Choose the part of the sentence that is grammatically incorrect:
_Unless_ (A) / _you do not pay attention_ (B) / _to what I say_ (C), / _you will not succeed_ (D).
explanationEnglish: 'Unless' already possesses a negative meaning ('if not'). Adding a secondary negative auxiliary phrase ('do not') produces a double negative error.
subject: English

qEnglish: Choose the correct option for correcting the following sentence: 'If it rains, I would not go to the park.'
optionsEnglish: I was not going to the park if it rains.
I would not be going to the park if it rains.
I should not go to the park if it rains.
If it rains, I will not go to the park.
explanationEnglish: A first conditional structure requires an alignment consisting of a simple present condition ('If it rains') paired with a simple future consequence ('I will not go').
subject: English

qEnglish: Choose the correct option to make the following into a single coherent conditional sentence: 'You can't get in you don't have the tickets.'
optionsEnglish: You can't get in whether you have the tickets.
You don't have the tickets you can't get in.
You can't get in if you don't have the tickets.
You can't get in, you don't have tickets.
explanationEnglish: Connecting the consequence clause with its necessary premise requires the clear insertion of the conditional conjunction 'if'.
subject: English

qEnglish: If I had known you were in hospital, I _______ to visit you.
explanationEnglish: The third conditional clause 'If I had known' must be resolved via a main clause built around 'would have + past participle'.
subject: English

qEnglish: Put the verb into the correct form: If I _______ (know) his number, I would phone him.
explanationEnglish: The presence of 'would phone' inside the primary clause points out a Type 2 conditional sentence, which takes a simple past verb ('knew').
subject: English

qEnglish: If you _______ (tell) we had run out of bread, I _______ (buy) some.
explanationEnglish: To represent a hypothetical past scenario that didn't happen, a Type 3 conditional frame is used: past perfect ('had told') and future perfect in the past ('would have bought').
subject: English

qEnglish: If you _______ hard, you will drill the nail in.
explanationEnglish: A first conditional structure contains a simple present tense verb phrase ('hit') inside its dependent if-clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: Unless you apologise _______.
explanationEnglish: 'Unless' creates a negative future condition, requiring a logical simple future consequence clause ('I will punish you').
subject: English

qEnglish: If he had worked hard, he _______.
explanationEnglish: The clause condition 'had worked' sets a Type 3 format requiring 'would have passed' inside the main text statement.
subject: English

qEnglish: If I _______ a bird I would fly in the sky.
explanationEnglish: Hypothetical, counterfactual statements use the past subjunctive verb form 'were' for all subject pronouns.
subject: English

qEnglish: Even if you don't work for me I _______ your work.
explanationEnglish: A present condition introduced by 'even if' takes a matching simple future verb expression 'will do' in its declarative main clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: If I _______ a doctor, I would serve the poor.
explanationEnglish: An imaginary situation in the present uses 'were' in the conditional clause to show it is a counterfactual condition.
subject: English

qEnglish: Had you woken up earlier, you _______ seen a beautiful sunrise.
explanationEnglish: The inverted conditional clause 'Had you woken' demands the matching continuation component 'would have' to satisfy Type 3 patterns.
subject: English

qEnglish: Until he _______ his work, he will not be allowed to go out.
explanationEnglish: Time clauses introduced by 'until' use the simple present tense ('finishes') to describe a future condition.
subject: English

qEnglish: Unless he _______ harder, he will not succeed.
explanationEnglish: The introductory clause word 'Unless' is followed by a regular simple present form ('tries') when paired with a simple future consequence clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: _______ they left early, they would have reached by now.
explanationEnglish: To initiate an inverted past conditional structure that matches the result 'would have reached', the clause must begin with 'Had'.
subject: English

qEnglish: If it rains, the college will _______ closed.
explanationEnglish: The modal auxiliary 'will' must be followed by the base dictionary form of the verb, which is 'be'.
subject: English

qEnglish: If I _______ wealthy, I should have many friends.
explanationEnglish: An imaginary or unreal condition in the present takes the past subjunctive form 'were'.
subject: English

qEnglish: If I _______ attended the training course, I would have learnt something.
explanationEnglish: A Type 3 conditional statement pairs 'would have learnt' with a past perfect clause introduced by 'had'.
subject: English

qEnglish: If we had taken a coach, it _______ better.
explanationEnglish: The condition 'had taken' establishes a Type 3 pattern, requiring the past perfect modal frame 'would have been'.
subject: English

qEnglish: If we _______ the car, we would have the problem of where to park.
explanationEnglish: The presence of 'would have' (simple would + base verb have) in the main clause shows it is a Type 2 conditional sentence, requiring the simple past form 'took'.
subject: English

qEnglish: If I were an actress, I _______ Hollywood films too.
explanationEnglish: The subclause condition 'were' denotes a Type 2 imaginary scenario, which resolves through 'would + base verb' ('would sign').
subject: English

qEnglish: If I had told the truth, _______ punishment.
explanationEnglish: A conditional clause in the past perfect ('had told') requires a main clause in the future perfect-in-the-past form ('would have escaped').
subject: English

qEnglish: If we _______ a lottery, we will buy a car.
explanationEnglish: A first conditional sentence features a simple future main clause ('will buy') and a simple present conditional clause ('win').
subject: English

qEnglish: If the result of the admission test _______ today, she _______ a telegram.
explanationEnglish: A standard type-1 open future conditional structure links a simple present passive condition ('is announced') to a simple future outcome ('will send').
subject: English

qEnglish: If you had worked hard, you _______.
explanationEnglish: The past perfect condition 'had worked' indicates a Type 3 conditional sentence, which requires the main clause form 'would have passed'.
subject: English

qEnglish: If you make a promise, you must be sure to _______.
explanationEnglish: According to the official exam answer key provided, option (A) 'accomplish' is the designated correct response.
subject: English

qEnglish: Fill in the blank with the appropriate conditional clause: He must be lying _______.
optionsEnglish: if he was told you that
if he will tell you that
explanationEnglish: The simple past phrase 'if he told you that' forms a grammatically correct conditional clause to complement the present statement.
subject: English

qEnglish: If he had called me, I _______ helped him.
explanationEnglish: The third conditional pattern ('If + past perfect') requires 'would have' followed by a past participle in the main clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: If they doubled the price of petrol, there _______ so much traffic on the road.
explanationEnglish: The simple past verb 'doubled' sets up a second conditional context, which requires 'wouldn't be' in the main clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: Choose the most appropriate subordinate clause to fill in the blank: I can never catch up with him _______.
optionsEnglish: No matter how hard I try
No matter how hard I will try
explanationEnglish: The adverbial phrase 'No matter how hard I try' correctly uses the simple present tense to express a concession.
subject: English

qEnglish: If I _______ (get) this job, I _______ (buy) a new car by next year.
explanationEnglish: The timeline target 'by next year' combined with a simple present condition ('get') requires the future perfect tense 'will have bought' in the main clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: Identify the grammatically correct sentence from the options below:
optionsEnglish: Was I a King, I would lived a life of ease.
Were I a King, I would live a life of ease.
Were I a King, I would had lived a life of ease.
Were I a King, I lived a life of ease.
explanationEnglish: An inverted second conditional clause ('Were I a King') correctly takes a main clause consisting of 'would + base verb' ('would live').
subject: English

qEnglish: We _______ go provided the weather _______ fine.
explanationEnglish: The conditional connector 'provided' acts like 'if'. It connects a simple present clause ('is') to a principal future clause ('shall go').
subject: English

qEnglish: You _______ succeeded if you _______ harder.
explanationEnglish: A classic type-3 conditional construction pairs an 'if + past perfect' clause ('had tried') with a 'would have + past participle' main clause.
subject: English

qEnglish: Unless it _______ today, we _______ not play tennis.
explanationEnglish: The word 'Unless' introduces a condition in the simple present ('is fine'), which takes a corresponding future modal expression ('shall') in the main clause.
subject: English
